name: Wadjet
description: Wadjet, also known as Uraeus, is one of the oldest deities of Egypt. She was originally the local goddess of the city of Dep (later Buto), which became part of Lower Egypt. Wadjet was considered the protector of Lower Egypt, and later became the patron and protector of all of Egypt. She is often depicted as a cobra, specifically the Egyptian cobra, ready to strike, or as a woman with the head of a cobra. Her image adorns the crowns of the pharaohs, symbolizing her protection and divine authority. Wadjet is associated with royalty, protection, healing, and Lower Egypt. As a protector goddess, she defends against enemies and evil forces. She is also associated with the sun and fire, often depicted with solar imagery. Her power extends to healing, as she was believed to possess medicinal knowledge and the ability to cure snake bites. She is a powerful symbol of royal authority and divine protection, safeguarding the pharaohs and the land of Egypt from harm. Wadjet's vigilance and power were crucial for maintaining order and stability in the kingdom, making her one of the most revered and important deities in the Egyptian pantheon. Her role as a protector and symbol of power ensured her enduring significance throughout Egyptian history.
subtitle: Protector of Lower Egypt and Pharaohs

keyMyths.title: Wadjet's Protection of Horus
keyMyths.description: Wadjet protected the infant Horus from Set in the marshes of the Delta after Osiris' death. She hid him and nurtured him until he was old enough to avenge his father.

keyMyths.title: Wadjet and the Pharaoh's Crown
keyMyths.description: Wadjet became a symbol of royal authority and protection when she was placed on the pharaoh's crown as the Uraeus. Her presence ensured the pharaoh's safety and divine right to rule.

keyMyths.title: Wadjet as a Fire Goddess
keyMyths.description: In some myths, Wadjet is associated with fire and the sun, representing her ability to destroy enemies and protect against evil. Her fiery gaze could ward off any threat to the pharaoh and Egypt.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Detailed descriptions of Egyptian deities and their roles.
sources.text: Detailed descriptions of Egyptian deities and their roles.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and precious items were made to Wadjet in her temples. These offerings were intended to appease the goddess and ensure her continued protection.

worshipPractices.name: Statues and Amulets
worshipPractices.description: Statues and amulets of Wadjet, often in the form of a cobra, were created and worn for protection against evil and to invoke the goddess's power. These were common among pharaohs and commoners alike.

festivals.name: Feast of Wadjet
festivals.timing: Celebrated on the 25th day of Peret
festivals.description: The Feast of Wadjet was a significant festival dedicated to the goddess, celebrated with processions, offerings, and rituals to honor her role as protector of Lower Egypt and the pharaoh.

cultural.modernLegacy: Wadjet's image endures in modern Egyptology and popular culture, symbolizing ancient Egyptian royalty and divine protection. She is frequently depicted in art, literature, and media related to ancient Egypt.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Wadjet's origins trace back to the predynastic era, making her one of Egypt's oldest deities. She was initially the local goddess of Dep, later known as Buto, a city in the Nile Delta. Buto served as an important religious and political center in Lower Egypt, and Wadjet's influence grew in tandem with the city's prominence. According to mythology, Wadjet was born from the primordial waters of the Nile Delta, embodying the fertile and protective aspects of the region. Her association with the cobra, a venomous snake native to the area, further solidified her role as a guardian figure. As the protector of Buto, she was responsible for safeguarding its inhabitants and ensuring the prosperity of the land. Her early worship involved local rituals and offerings aimed at securing her favor and protection against threats both natural and supernatural. This early veneration laid the foundation for her later role as a national deity.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most prominent myths involving Wadjet is her role as a protector of Horus, the falcon-headed god and son of Isis and Osiris. After Osiris was murdered by his brother Set, Isis hid the infant Horus in the marshes of the Nile Delta to protect him from Set's wrath. Wadjet, along with other deities, guarded and nurtured Horus, ensuring his survival until he was old enough to avenge his father's death and claim his rightful place as pharaoh. This story highlights Wadjet's protective nature and her association with royalty and divine succession. Another myth recounts how Wadjet used her fiery gaze to defeat enemies of the pharaoh and protect the land from invaders. Her cobra form, poised to strike, symbolized her readiness to defend Egypt against any threat. These myths reinforced Wadjet's image as a powerful and benevolent goddess, deeply intertwined with the fate of Egypt and its rulers.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Wadjet's worship was centered in Buto, where a grand temple was dedicated to her. This temple served as a focal point for religious ceremonies and festivals honoring the goddess. Priests and priestesses of Wadjet oversaw the daily rituals, which included offerings of food, drink, and precious objects. Statues and amulets of Wadjet, often depicting her as a cobra or a woman with a cobra head, were created and venerated as representations of her divine presence. These objects were believed to possess protective powers and were worn by pharaohs and commoners alike. As Wadjet's influence grew, her cult spread throughout Egypt, and she became associated with the pharaoh and the concept of divine kingship. Her image was often incorporated into royal regalia, such as the Uraeus, the cobra emblem worn on the pharaoh's crown, symbolizing his authority and protection. The annual Feast of Wadjet was a major celebration that drew people from all over Egypt to honor the goddess and seek her blessings.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Wadjet's legacy extends far beyond her role as a local goddess of Buto. As the protector of Lower Egypt and later the entire nation, she became a symbol of Egyptian royalty, divine authority, and protection. Her image as the Uraeus, the cobra on the pharaoh's crown, served as a constant reminder of the pharaoh's power and his responsibility to safeguard the land and its people. Wadjet's influence can be seen in various aspects of Egyptian art, architecture, and religious beliefs. Her association with the eye of Ra, the sun god, further solidified her role as a powerful and benevolent deity. Even after the decline of ancient Egyptian civilization, Wadjet's image continued to resonate, inspiring artists, writers, and scholars throughout history. Today, she remains a prominent figure in Egyptology and popular culture, representing the enduring legacy of ancient Egyptian mythology and the enduring power of its goddesses.

shortDescription: Wadjet, the cobra goddess, was the protector of Lower Egypt and later all of Egypt. She symbolized royalty, divine authority, and protection.
